video_title: Can Finance Be A Tool for Good? with Matthew Monahan | TGS 226
gist: Nate Hagens interviews Matthew Monahan, co-founder of Ma Earth and Biome Trust, on regenerative finance: why only 2-4% of global philanthropy reaches environmental/nature causes, and how Ma Earth's rounds-based quadratic-funding platform (built on an open, wallet-less AT Protocol data layer called Certified) tries to route small grassroots grants directly to land-stewardship projects worldwide. The conversation also covers why Monahan sees Ethereum (not Bitcoin) as the right infrastructure for nature-linked finance, and his own doubts about whether financializing nature can avoid reproducing the extractive logic it's meant to fix.

insights.claim: Only 2-4% of global philanthropic giving (out of hundreds of billions of dollars donated annually) goes to environment, nature, and climate-related causes, a figure corroborated by multiple studies across different countries.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest (Ma Earth co-founder)
insights.so_what: Marks environmental/regenerative causes as a structurally underfunded niche within global philanthropy, suggesting outsized room for new capital allocators focused specifically on nature.
insights.quote: only two to four percent of global philanthropy goes into environment, nature, climate-related causes

insights.claim: Top-down climate mechanisms such as new carbon-credit classes, net-zero corporate pledges, and COP declarations largely fail to reach grassroots actors, such as a parent trying to fund a school garden, because the resourcing never flows down to that level.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Signals a structural inefficiency in climate-finance distribution that grassroots-first funding platforms are positioned to exploit as a gap.

insights.claim: Monahan identifies three structural reasons resources fail to reach frontline regenerative work: funding systems are top-down and one-size-fits-all despite every location's ecology being different; philanthropy, impact investing, government funding and competing nonprofits operate in separate, siloed pools with no shared information layer; and there is a base-layer trust deficit because funders rarely see a feedback loop showing what their money actually changed.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete three-part checklist for evaluating any new climate/regenerative-finance platform or startup: does it decentralize decision-making, unify siloed capital pools, and create verifiable feedback loops?

insights.claim: Grassroots regenerative activity (tree planting, beach cleanups, community gardens) already happens without funding because it is driven by intrinsic motivation, and Monahan's experience is that these groups mainly need small amounts of capital to get 'unblocked' rather than large grants.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Implies a high-leverage opportunity for climate philanthropy in small, unblocking micro-grants rather than concentrating capital in a few large institutional grants.

insights.claim: Ma Earth runs regenerative land-project funding in six-month rounds: a cohort of projects is selected, each runs a three-week community crowdfunding campaign, and philanthropic matching funds are then distributed based on the crowdfunding results using a quadratic-funding-style mechanism.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A specific, replicable capital-allocation design that ties community demand signal directly to matching-fund distribution, relevant as a template for other climate/regen-finance platforms.
insights.quote: we're starting with this notion of quadratic funding

insights.claim: Ma Earth's current (July 2026) round three received over 800 applications from 101 countries, selected roughly 175 projects from more than 60 countries, and is backing them with a $1 million matching pool after the pool was increased late thanks to contributors including Imaginal Seeds.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Concrete traction data point for assessing the platform's scale and growth relative to its stated ambitions.
insights.quote: we received over 800 applications from 101 countries

insights.claim: The average matching grant on Ma Earth's round-three platform is roughly $5,000 per project, explicitly not enough to cover major costs like staffing or land acquisition, but intended to create visibility and momentum through the fact that ~175 projects fundraise simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations for grant-size economics on micro-grant platforms; the value proposition is collective visibility and momentum, not balance-sheet-scale capital per project.
insights.quote: the average grant size is gonna be roughly $5,000 in matching funds

insights.claim: Ma Earth grew out of two 2024 pilot funding rounds run on Gitcoin, an open-source-software crowdfunding tool, making it one of the first efforts to apply Gitcoin-style quadratic funding to regenerative land-stewardship projects rather than software development.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Shows a technology-transfer pattern where funding mechanisms proven in open-source software grants are being repurposed for environmental finance, a template other builders could copy.
insights.quote: the pilots were on a platform called Gitcoin, G not B, Gitcoin

insights.claim: Across its first two rounds, Ma Earth deployed roughly $400,000 into about 50 projects worldwide; separately, Monahan's family philanthropic vehicle Biome Trust has given roughly $10 million in grants over its lifetime, and investment into the Mangaroa Farms regenerative-agriculture project in New Zealand totals around $20 million.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Gives a baseline for the current capital scale behind Monahan's regenerative-finance ecosystem, useful for sizing the space relative to his 10,000x growth ambition.

insights.claim: Monahan's stated ambition for Ma Earth is to scale matched-funding volume roughly 10,000x from current levels, aiming to move 'many billions of dollars' through the platform's infrastructure, though he frames this with explicit uncertainty about whether it will succeed.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Sets a north-star benchmark against which to judge whether the underlying Certified/AT Protocol tech stack could plausibly support institutional-scale capital flows.
insights.quote: we're thinking in terms of, like, another 10,000X on those numbers

insights.claim: Ma Earth plans to launch its fourth funding round on Earth Day 2027, following the round-three cycle that began July 1, 2026 and runs through July 21, 2026, indicating a roughly annual cadence for the platform's funding cycle.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete forward date for tracking whether the platform executes on its stated scaling roadmap.

themes.theme: Portable Trust Infrastructure Behind the Platform

insights.claim: Ma Earth's underlying technology, referred to as 'Certified,' is stewarded by the Hypercerts Foundation and is designed so a project's application data, impact claims, and updates remain sovereign to that project and portable across other funding platforms rather than owned by Ma Earth itself.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Signals a broader shift toward portable, project-owned impact-verification data layers, analogous to open banking, relevant for assessing moats in impact-verification infrastructure.
insights.quote: platform underneath which has been stewarded by a group called Hypercerts Foundation, the certified

insights.claim: Monahan's colleague describes the repeated grant-application and impact-reporting cycle nonprofits face across multiple funders as 'bureaucratic violence,' and Ma Earth is designing its data-commons layer specifically to let a project's profile, images, and impact claims move with it across the ecosystem instead of being re-entered for every new funder.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Frames grant-application friction itself as a solvable infrastructure problem, a concrete pain point any competing impact-funding platform would need to address.
insights.quote: bureaucratic violence in the current funding ecosystem

insights.claim: Ma Earth built its identity/onboarding layer on the AT Protocol (the decentralized social-networking protocol that came out of Bluesky) instead of traditional crypto wallets, so onboarding only requires an email address and a six-digit one-time code, deliberately avoiding wallets or seed phrases.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete UX pattern -- decentralized-but-walletless onboarding -- that other Web3-adjacent impact/fintech products could copy to cross the usability barrier that has limited blockchain-based giving platforms.
insights.quote: Just type in an email address, get a six-digit OTP code

themes.theme: Ethereum vs. Bitcoin as Regenerative-Finance Infrastructure

insights.claim: Monahan characterizes Bitcoin as essentially 'digital gold' -- a static store of value that mimics gold's properties but cannot host applications or programmable logic -- distinct from the smart-contract capability he thinks a nature-linked financial system needs.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A specific, arguable thesis for anyone weighing BTC vs. ETH exposure through a regenerative/ESG-finance lens -- Bitcoin's store-of-value narrative is framed as irrelevant to programmable nature-finance use cases.
insights.quote: You can't really do anything with Bitcoin. It just sits there.

insights.claim: Monahan argues that public smart-contract blockchains, specifically Ethereum, are currently the only infrastructure capable of supporting a nature-linked complementary-currency or token system at scale, because of its programmability versus Bitcoin's narrower design.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Positions Ethereum specifically (not crypto broadly) as the relevant asset/infrastructure to watch for anyone tracking regenerative-finance or nature-token experiments.
insights.quote: Ethereum is a completely different animal.

insights.claim: Monahan credits the Ethereum community with repeatedly prioritizing long-term censorship-resistance, open-source design, privacy, and security over short-term financial interest, calling it 'one of the most courageous pieces of technology' he has observed in his career, despite having no formal affiliation with the Ethereum ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: An outside, non-crypto-native observer's strong endorsement of Ethereum's governance culture specifically (not its price), useful context for its long-term protocol-risk profile.
insights.quote: Ethereum to me is one of the most courageous pieces of technology that I've ever seen.

insights.claim: Monahan estimates that roughly 95% of public discourse about blockchain technology centers on financial speculation and trading, implying non-speculative uses -- like decentralized trust/verification infrastructure for grant funding -- remain a small, underexplored niche.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Frames non-financial blockchain applications (verification, attestation, data commons) as a contrarian, underserved use case relative to the dominant speculative narrative.
insights.quote: ninety-five percent of the time people are talking about blockchains, it's somehow related to making money and financial speculation and trading

themes.theme: Carbon-Credit Perversions and the Risk of Financializing Nature

insights.claim: Monahan says the carbon-credit incentive structure on the regenerative farm his organization stewards in New Zealand actively damaged ecological outcomes, because it financially rewarded planting whichever tree species cheaply satisfied a carbon-sequestration accounting table rather than the species actually suited to that ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete cautionary case for investors evaluating nature-based carbon-offset projects: top-down eco-credit accounting can produce species/land-use mismatches that undermine the stated ecological goal.
insights.quote: our carbon credit incentive is so messed up

insights.claim: Because of that carbon-credit experience, Monahan deliberately avoided building Ma Earth around biodiversity or eco-credit markets, instead starting with philanthropic gifts and grants where communities themselves define goals and metrics rather than a standardized top-down accounting table, an approach he credits partly to lessons from Regen Network's work.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Signals practitioner-level skepticism toward biodiversity/eco-credit markets as a near-term scalable asset class, worth weighing against more bullish nature-credit market narratives.

insights.claim: Monahan acknowledges an unresolved risk that any system for pricing and financializing nature -- including his own -- could simply perpetuate the same growth and commodification logic that caused ecological damage in the first place, and admits he has not yet found a way to fully resolve that tension.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Flags a structural, unresolved risk in the entire regenerative-finance/nature-token thesis that should temper assumptions these markets scale cleanly or without side effects.

themes.theme: Ecological Overshoot and the Wealth Illusion

insights.claim: Monahan cites a prior guest (Brett Concannon) for the estimate that Earth's annual regenerative capacity is roughly 50% lower today than it used to be, attributing the decline to human impact on land and forest systems.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A stark ecological baseline metric underlying the urgency case for reallocating capital toward regenerative land projects.

insights.claim: Citing WWF data, Monahan notes that over 70% of wild mammal populations have vanished within his and Nate Hagens's lifetimes, framing this as biodiversity loss on a timescale unprecedented after hundreds of millions of years of evolution.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A widely citable, sourced statistic for framing the scale of biodiversity collapse when making the case for regenerative-finance capital allocation.
insights.quote: 70% plus of wild mammals' populations have vanished

insights.claim: Monahan frames concentrated global wealth as 'digital claims on biophysical and ecological reality of the future,' arguing that as ecological systems decline, they will increasingly fail to support the financial asset values built on top of them -- an 'Earth clock' ticking down on that wealth.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A systemic tail-risk framing for long-horizon allocators: paper wealth and underlying real-resource capacity can diverge, which is worth incorporating into long-duration portfolio thinking.
insights.quote: What is all that extreme wealth? It's really digital claims on biophysical and ecological reality of the future.

themes.theme: Bottom-Up Systems Change and Bridging the Tech/Environmental Divide

insights.claim: Monahan's strategy favors building regenerative economic patterns bottom-up -- starting at the local/farm level, then scaling to bioregional and watershed governance -- rather than waiting for top-down policy or currency reform, while still believing both top-down and bottom-up change are necessary in parallel.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Suggests capital deployed at local/bioregional scale (like Ma Earth's model) may compound into larger systemic change faster than waiting on national policy reform.

insights.claim: Monahan does not expect fiat national currencies to be replaced within his lifetime or in generations he can foresee, and explicitly rejects the idea of discarding existing currencies for Bitcoin; his goal is redesigning money and finance so they serve ecological life, not replacing money as a coordinating technology outright.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Positions his work as reformist rather than a Bitcoin-maximalist or currency-replacement thesis, useful context when evaluating his Ethereum-positive but Bitcoin-skeptical stance.
insights.quote: I'm not at all suggesting, like, throw out the current currencies and we're gonna replace it with Bitcoin or anything like that

insights.claim: Monahan sees a persistent cultural gap between technologists, who exhibit what he calls 'nature blindness,' and environmentalists, who often lack appreciation for the pace of exponential technologies like AI, robotics, and genomics, and argues regenerative-finance work requires bridging both communities rather than favoring one.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Implies regenerative-finance and climate-tech builders need literacy in both ecological long-range thinking and frontier-tech pace, a two-sided skill gap worth screening for in this space.
insights.quote: there's this nature blindness that is really frustrating

insights.claim: Ma Earth charges no platform fee, operates as a nonprofit, and does not offer equity or stock-option-style compensation to collaborators, explicitly positioning itself as mission-funded infrastructure rather than a venture-backed startup.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: Clarifies the organization's incentive structure and business model for anyone assessing its durability, talent-recruitment approach, or conflicts of interest relative to for-profit climate-fintech competitors.
insights.quote: We don't offer stock options and pre-IPO shares of the latest AI company

insights.claim: Two mountains -- Taranaki Maunga (Mount Taranaki) in Aotearoa New Zealand, following a treaty settlement between the Crown and Maori peoples, and Pyramid Mountain in the United States, via work from Regenerative Earth, the Center for Democratic Environmental Rights, and the Center for Ethical Land Transition -- have each been granted legal personhood and can steward their own funds, and both are active fundraising participants in Ma Earth's round three.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete, verifiable example of rights-of-nature legal structures intersecting with crowdfunding infrastructure, relevant to anyone tracking the legal-personhood-for-nature trend as it meets finance.

insights.claim: Farmers on Crutches, a Congo-based nonprofit that trains war amputees to farm and build livelihoods on crutches, was funded through Ma Earth via a referral from Be the Earth Foundation, illustrating how the platform's grant flow can reach small, previously unconnected grassroots organizations.
insights.speaker: Matthew Monahan, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete example demonstrating the platform's ability to source and fund highly specific, under-resourced grassroots organizations that would be unlikely to reach traditional institutional funders.
